speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 26th, 1997. And we are doing an interview with Mr. Semyon Menyuk. The interviewer is Rae Meltzer. And we are doing this in Gaithersburg, Maryland, in the United States of America. And we will be speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Rae Meltzer. And today is February 26th, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Semyon Menyuk. The interview is being conducted in Gaithersburg, Maryland, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Menyuk, will you give us your full name, first and last, and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name is Semyon, S-E-M-Y-O-N. And the last name is Menyuk, M-E-N-Y-U-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Was that the name that was given to you when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the Yiddish name. But they give me these two names together. It's [? Royal ?] [? Borough. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, is this a Hebrew-- if to translate this in Hebrew-- in English will be now, bless you. Bless God Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, mhm. All right. Did you ever have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me the date of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 4 of May, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it's the 75th year. In May will be 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And please spell the name of the city and the country where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city, Komarow. That's a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-M-O-R-O-V-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, to start, I would like to suggest that you begin to tell us what you remember from your childhood in Komarow, as far back as you can remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we was in-- Komarow, it was a village. Approximately, it was about 250 houses. Knowing my parents was born in the same corner of the village my grandfather, and then-- and more and more and more, all the generations was in Komarow. It was-- most of them, it's Ukraine. We live only that's five, six families Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the beginning, I was in school. Now, then, now I can say what-- what I was doing. I was study in school. Now, and I helped the parents, what they can do. And after there when I got finished maybe four grades, and the parents send me to this. We didn't got more. It's only four grades in the village. Now, it was this-- they teach us Ukraine and Polish and no Yiddish. Then the parents decided to send me in a school where it's-- where we can study Yiddish and Hebrew and the [INAUDIBLE]. And we got in Sarny, city Sarny, my mother's sister. And they send me to stay with them. And I went to school Tarbut. I never studied in Tarbut. Then I came that's-- I finished the school Tarbut in 1938. And I come to the parents back. And I helped the parents in village Komarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So when you came back, you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: This approximately that's in 1938. That was maybe 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to work to help your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I worked. The parents got like a small farm, and not a lot of land. A little land that we got. A car we got. A horse we got. Now at home, everything single vegetables the mother was growing. Now, and I was helping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, mhm. Politically, were things quiet in 1938 in Komarow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- oh, excuse me, please. We got only problems and when I was in [? Sarny ?] we got problems when we was in school. That's, I think, it's belong more political. But until 1935, for Jewish people, it was not bad in Poland. It was no anti-Semitism. And begin after the 1935. And it was the-- no, the president, he died, Pilsudski, it become the anti-Semitism more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I want to say. We begin to go to school. We was fighting. The Polish people was fighting with us. We was went to school in the Hebrew school, and they went in the Polish school. And we, every day, we got fights. But they didn't like us, but we Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Was that in Komarow or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Sarny.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sarny.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Komarow, most of them that was Ukraine, no, we didn't feel something bad, nothing what I can-- all the-- we was playing with the children, Ukraine children and was living in Komarow together, never with some problems, political problems. Most of them, it was with the Polish children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when I was in school in Sarny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. So when you came back home, you were 16, did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you continue to stay and help your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's until the 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, it begin a different life for us. That's was the war, begin the war between Germany and Poland. And Germany occupied the-- a piece Poland. And they [INAUDIBLE] occupied that piece Poland as we begin to leave by the Russians from the 1939 from approximately from September month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, here begin a new life. When it's beginning the new life, now, I didn't know very well the Russian language because we didn't study in the schools Russian language. We studied Polish. We studied Yiddish. We studied Hebrew. We studied religion. No, not Russian. Is in beginning when Russian come, my father begin to start teach me little Russian because he was by the [? Tsar, ?] and he's in the army. And he was now, like, I now, I understand. But he was a good soldier. And he was in the war when it was with the Germans. He was a military prisoner, I think. The [INAUDIBLE] in Germany. And he was maybe one year in Germany. Now then he comes to Russia. And he knows the Russian language. And he got a lot Russian for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medals. That's what the Russian-- the Russian Tsar for the Jewish people, they didn't give the-- how it's called-- the no medals. And he got, I think, what that's-- he was a good, a good military man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's what he being to study me the Russian language. And then I went to school to study more Russian. And I begin-- begin to work. My work I begin that was a-- we called it a cooperative. That's business, government business. I didn't got experience in this business. No, it was a Russian nice man, I will say, that's what I remember. He begin to start to teach me how to work, what you need to do. And now, and I was young. It's 17 years and no experience. Now, in what more the-- that was the people need to [? work ?] for this for my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he comes now, it's like a democratic place was they came. And like he said what I'm-- and the people knows me. And I would begin to work with that [? Tsar ?] like, now, the head of the government business. It was maybe a five, six villages belong to that-- to that village. No, and I was working very, very good, and more was I working, more it was better for me. And the business was good. And the boss, when I came the main-- main office, it was in Kolki. And now, and I came sometimes as they was laughing for me one time, a young boy, and I take a job like this. And most of them older people. And I was feeling very, very bad, what happened. No, for from my respect, I need to respect them, like older, older people. And they was looking of me like a child, Not the boy-- most of them, if the work is good, and then it's go nicely, they would tell me that you will were very good. I was happy. And that's I was working until it's begin the war with Russia, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Knowing when this begin the work, the war, 1941, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me just a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What product was this cooperative?

speaker: interviewee
text: The store? The co-op? That was all kind stores. We got hardware stores. We got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --new clothes. We got all produce to eat. Everything, everything in metal. It was no-- well, universal-- universal stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the village, it's only-- only one store--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry I interrupted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --things changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when it's become-- begin the war, I was in that time in Komarow. And the people, the stores, what they was they sell there, they brought the money. They need to bring to me. And I take them to the bank to Kolki or to [? Manevich. ?] Now, must send to Kolki. When it's come the war, they have the wars. We hear that's in [? no. ?] It was an aerodrome near to us. We hear what its bombs. And then the radio says that it's a war. This morning, I was-- I want to go to take the money to the bank now, like usual. We need to every two days I was take the money to the bank. And people from the-- from my village, they saw me what I am going to Kolki, you know. They know what I'm going to take the money because it's always the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people come to me and says, you know what? Don't go now in to Kolki because we saw some people with-- with guns in the forest. Now, if like that's how I decided not to go, not to go. And I came at home, back, and I stayed. Then worse, and worse, and worse, and I didn't go more. Now, and we stayed. And we hear what the Germans come all the time nearer and nearer to us, the pass the border what between German-- German and between Poland. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. Now, they take from Poland the borders. They move them. Then in approximately in that two weeks, we've got the Germans, the army generals in our places. That was the first day, the first begin, the first struggles. We was-- and that time a lot of people went to-- want to have evacuated from this place and go to Russia more.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- we was talking at home with my father. He tell-- he tell me and tell to the family, you know what? We will go. I was in Germany. In that time, the Germans was very good to Jewish people. No, I think we didn't do something wrong for the people, the Ukraine people will kill us or do bad things to us. Never he believed it what's is going to happen to Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we all of them, the family no experience for life and experience. No, we was listen to the father what he said. And we did. And we stayed. And then when the Germans came, my father was speaking very well German. It's happened, but it was a conversation between an older German soldier with the father. He told the father-- the father told him that I a Jew. And it's happened, but it was a nice, nice German person. And he said to the father, you will have very bad time, very bad time. And maybe I don't know if you will survive from this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that was one person. After this, the father begin to talk with us. And he said what the German soldier tell us. Now, it was too late. We cannot go anywhere because the Germans is here. And if we will go from the village, out from the village, will be worse. The father said to us, you know what we will do? Let me move from home now. We will go in forest for a while until the forced armies will pass the places. Maybe later, will be more some government, maybe more protection from the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not happened that. We was-- we went. We got out now our horse and wagon. And we stay-- now that's we went from the-- in forest maybe for 5 kilometers. It's 5 or 7 kilometers down to [INAUDIBLE] forest. It's passed the couple days. We decided to come back to the place, to the house where-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- where we live. When we came back in a couple days, they robbed the house. They take everything from the house. It's empty at all. Only the stayed the walls. We came and we saw like it's empty. It's not possible to stay in that house. And in Komarow, was living my mom-- my mother's sister with a family too. They said, you don't have where to live. Come-- come to us, and we will live together. That was done. We begin to live in the this now. And every day is worse and worse. It's become the Germans passed. We didn't got afraid for the Germans like it begin the Ukraine. They begin to do terrible things to us. It comes-- they begin. They come at night time. They take the-- now, for-- I-- I want to say a couple moments what they-- what they done to us. They will come in shoot near the house at night time. Then they take the father and a couple more men and me. And they take us to the water. And they tell, go in the water with the clothes at night time. And they-- no, it's hard to tell you, to explain what they was doing without the Germans, without the Germans. That's-- the Germans, they passed. And the Germans stay in Kolki. That only in our village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the Ukrainians, yes. I-- I want to say, it's now, it's hard to say with all of them. It was not all of them. I will tell you later on. It's what-- no, it was the bad ones. And it was a couple good ones. Now, what the problem? We cannot say all of them is bad or all of them is good. Now, most of them become bad. And we, well, that's what they was doing. And then another night, they come to me and take from me, give give up the money what you got. They was-- they know what I was working for a, know that's in the government business, and I got the money, government money. At night time, give up the money. I give up all the money what I got. They asked me, give-- you have-- you have a gun, or you have-- I say I never got a gun, and I don't have now to. And I was not in the army that time. And I don't know how to use the gun. And I never got. No, that was only-- only to more, to bring some conflicts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and that-- we stayed like that. Every day is a new-- new-- new-- new problems to take our seats. It was a lot, a lot, a lot of them. It's-- I forgot a lot of them. Now, to tell you only the episodes, the short episodes, it's un-possible. Every day it was problems. After the problems with we got for a couple months what we stay in the village Komarow, decided the-- their head of their-- when the German came, the head of the village said we need to put-- to take, use-- the German tell to the ghetto, take us to the ghetto. Now, into the ghetto, we didn't take-- we didn't got what to take. They tell us, you can take only the food if you want. That's all. No-- was--

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHING] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We take what we can-- we-- what we take, most of them, a little food what we got. And we came to the ghetto to Kolki. And that's time when we went to the ghetto Kolki. They beat us. They are doing terrible things. Now, when we came to the ghetto, now, the ghetto become that was maybe in Kolki, maybe a 10 blocks around. And it's full with Jewish people. And they take us in our room where it's a small room. The take us five, six until to 10 persons in the room, no food, nothing with we can buy anywhere. No what, the food it was dry. Who got the clothes, we got a little. If you can exchange somewhere you got suit for, you give up for a piece of bread. For a-- a pound bread, you give the best-- the best suit. And I will no beside, beside that what they was doing, they begin to do more wrong things and bad things. They put us up front, a piece-- a piece of clothes that's yellow color. They big. That's approximately by, oh, by 6 inches the diameter. One piece-- [COUGHS] excuse me, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- one piece up front and the second piece in the back where they can see-- [COUGHS] excuse me. Where they can see but we Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came-- they at night time, at day time. They begin to shot in ghetto. They done what they want. They rob us. They now take us to work. The work, it was a different kind works-- work. Where they cannot send-- find to work from the Ukraine people, they send us. The work, we work in the farms. We work in the where the horses, they got the Germans, to clean the horses, to clean their bathrooms. Now, I tell you, it's every day, every day was-- now, most of them, I and my father, we begin to work in the farm. We was working. Now, at night time, we came at home. In the daytime, we was working the same. My mother and my sister, the youngest, she was here. Everybody was working. Nobody they give the food. It was one pound for the whole family if you're working. That's-- no one pound for a family for four person, it's nothing. I tell you that it was the no every day worse and worse and worse and worse, no food, no clothes. We give up all the clothes for some food. Now, in one day, --it was in 1942-- begin the worst-- the worst time for us in ghetto. One day, they take my father and me in the end of the day, and they put us and-- and maybe 40, 50 person more-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --and they put us in a warehouse from-- it's a type of warehouse where you cannot break to come out. And they locked out us. Now we was thinking was they will kill us. Everybody says, that's they locked up for us to kill us. We didn't know nothing more what is going to happen to us. And we don't know what is outside, what is-- what has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we stay all the night in this place. We talk between us what-- who can sleep? Nobody sleeps. They are hungry. Now, we're waiting for whether it was they will kill. We stay over night. By morning-- this was maybe by 10 o'clock in the morning, maybe 10:30 approximately from the morning-- they opened the warehouse, the door, and take us from the warehouse And they said-- and they said, you will go with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop here while we change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]
